data.title: Anheuser-Busch InBev: This Beer Giant Is Pouring Profits And Brewing Value
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD) continues to be a strong enterprise, with their latest 10-K report highlighting the robust revenue growth and margin expansion achieved in 2024.

A wide economic moat supported by unmatched cost advantages, superb brands and a global presence makes competing with the Belgian brewer a formidable task even for the likes of Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY) and Molson Coors (TAP).

The current valuation looks attractive, with a tangible 38% undervaluation potentially taking place. This suggests shares could present a great deep-value opportunity for patient long-term investors.

I therefore upgrade my rating to a Strong Buy and added more shares to my position.

Economic Moat & Business Overview

AB InBev is the world’s largest brewer both by revenue, hectolitres ((hl)) of beer brewed and by their total number of brands. The Belgian brewer currently holds a market share of about 30% with rivals Heineken and Molson Coors being their closest competitors.

The firm’s core operations are quite simple and ultimately exactly the same as those done by smaller brewers. First, AB InBev purchases barley which it processes into a cookable form which then gets fermented along with a host of other ingredients, depending on the beer.

Finally, the beers are pasteurised to extend shelf lives and stop the fermentation process from continuing, with the end product then being bottled, labelled and shipped off to customers.

Brewing beer is ultimately a simple business and, luckily for AB, one which is massively scalable. AB InBev accordingly brews beers in huge quantities at a time which allows the firm to minimise fixed costs relative to total production expenses, thus increasing the efficiency of their operations.

I’ve found the best way to analyse AB InBev’s relative competitiveness in cost against rivals is through the metrics of revenues and COGS per hl of beer brewed. In 2024, AB InBev brewed 576 million hl of beer, which means their revenue per hl was $104 while COGS per hl was just $46.4.

In comparison, Heineken’s revenue and COGS per hl were $105 and $82 while Molson’s were $82 and $51 respectively. Clearly, AB InBev’s scale affords the firm massive cost advantages in the brewing process.

Furthermore, the increased weighting premium brands have in their sales mix is helping to boost revenue per HL, which creates an even larger gross margin.

Brands such as Budweiser, Stella Artois, Michelob Ultra, Busch, Corona, Modelo (except in the North American market), Jupiler and Beck’s are all produced by AB InBev along with another 490 different beers sold across the globe.

When it comes to moatiness, I consider consumer discretionary consumable companies such as AB InBev to derive competitive advantages from intangible assets, cost advantages and network effects, with the firm clearly benefitting from all three.

The durable brands of beer brewed by AB InBev are ultimately what help drive sales, as without these banners, beer is basically a homogenous product. As a result, these brands create a wide economic moat for the firm, as the pricing power afforded by these brands is formidable and difficult to replicate.

AB InBev clearly enjoys this moat on both a global (thanks to massive names such as Budweiser, Corona and Stella Artois) scale, as well as on a local one with regional favourites.

The brewer also benefits from clear cost advantages as outlined with my COGS per hl metric, which means that the company can spend less on each bottle of beer brewed as compared to their rivals.

AB is increasingly focused on expanding their non-beer portfolio of drinks through expansion into the pre-made cocktail, hard seltzer and non-alcoholic energy drink markets. While revenue growth is quite fast, I remain cautious about assigning any moatiness to this segment, as I am unconvinced that these products benefit from the same scalability and brand reputation as the beer brands.

Finally, the Belgian brewing giant benefits from network effects, whereby the popularity of their products helps further drive increased market penetration through a positive feedback loop.

Many of their global brands have become what could be considered default options in pubs and bars across the globe. While these beers may not have as much pricing power as boutique rivals or spirits, the volume in sales achieved is what reduces COGS for AB InBev as a whole and thus benefits the brewer.

With these factors in mind, the AB clearly has a wide and durable economic moat that should allow the firm to generate excess returns on invested capital above their weighted average cost of capital in the long term.

Financial Analysis

Anheuser-Busch InBev is fundamentally a profitable and impressive enterprise. Gross margins of around 56% have been stable for the past decade, with operating income hovering around 26% for the same period.

Such a robust operating margin is wonderful to see, especially given the relatively large amount the company must spend on SG&A to maintain brand reputations.

Returns have been a little paltrier for AB in recent years with ROA, ROE and ROIC averaging about 3%, 7% and 5.5% respectively. These figures are much lower than the 7%, 19% and 11% earned by the company back in 2010-2016 before the SAB miller acquisition which saw the company take on significant debt and additional assets, equity and capital.

However, while progress has been slightly slower than desired (mainly due to the significant 6x leverage taken by the company to finance the deal), I am happy that profitability and returns are increasing and particularly impressed by the robust performance achieved by the company in 2024.

Full-year earnings were reported on 2 February, with the overall results being very positive. Revenue increased 2.7% YoY to $59.8 billion thanks to solid outperformance QoQ throughout the year, which itself was mostly due to an improved sales mix which saw customer favouring more premium offerings.

This pivot which the firm has dubbed “premiumisation” is a logical one in my opinion as the overall number of customers consuming alcohol appears to have plateaued. Brands such as Michelob Ultra, Corona and Stella Artois are leading this charge, with these beers attracting customers away from cheaper staples such as Budweiser and Busch.

Gross profits increased 5.4% YoY to $33 billion thanks to COGS only growing 0.5%. This phenomenon is quite usual for AB InBev (assuming no massive supply side inflationary pressure is present) due to the aforementioned scalability benefits associated with the business of brewing beer.

SG&A expenses decreased 2% YoY as AB InBev was able to reduce their marketing requirements while maintaining robust sales. Equally, from a qualitative perspective I like the new ad campaigns the firm has made with popular comedian Shane Gillis and the Bud Light brand.

I think that the long-term impacts from their rather poorly conceived 2023 Bud Light advertising have not materialised with the brand steadily regaining popularity in North America.

Operating income increased a whopping 12% as a result of these profitability improvements, with the firm generating a pretty huge $15.5 billion in EBIT in 2024. Assuming a pretty accurate 22% tax rate suggests, the firm made about $12.1 billion in NOPAT through the last fiscal year.

Cash from operations totalled $15.1 billion which is superb and, importantly, well ahead of earnings. This ability for AB InBev to generate cash from operations illustrates how effectively management continues to run the enterprise, with an emphasis on maximising the gap between payables and receivables.

Seeking Alpha’s quant supports my hypothesis that 2024 was a bountiful year for AB with the current profitability grade being an excellent “A+”.

Turning to the firm’s balance sheet I am happy to report that the company continues to reduce debt levels through maturity repayments which provides a plausible pathway for the brewer to increase capital returns in the future.

Total current assets of $23 billion are still about $10 billion short of total current liabilities although the massive unlevered FCF of $11 billion helps plug this shortfall. Accounts payables constitute the majority of current liabilities which is to be expected considering their 365 days payables metric.

Long-term debt continues to decrease with the firm reducing their burden from $105 billion in 2018 to $84 billion in 2020 and now just $61 billion, down $7 billion YoY. The average interest rate of debts appears to be around 4% which is excellent given the higher rate environment now impacting most firms.

Interest expense has continued to fall accordingly by $1.1 billion (now just $2.85 billion a year) which implies a coverage ratio of 5.4x which is excellent.

I think the key to future returns for BUD stock lies in Anheuser-Busch InBev reducing their debts further so that capital can instead be deployed into additional share repurchases and dividends. With a view to the coming five years, I find it plausible to suggest the firm may increase their rate of cash returns (dividends and share buybacks) to shareholders as a proportion of NOPAT from the current 50% rate closer to 80%.

Given the colossal size of the brewer, I don’t think many future acquisitions would help the company to tangibly improve either their moat or profitability, which leads me to believe that capital would be best put to use buying back stock.

Considering the company as a whole, I believe rational decision-making, excellent capital allocation and a better management of brands is helping to improve earnings at the company.

The upcoming 2025 fiscal year should see much of the same occurring at the company, with management expecting EBIT growth of around 4-8% with revenues expected to remain roughly flat due to the weaker consumer spending environment in North America and Asia.

Valuation

I find Seeking Alpha’s Valuation tab to be a great place to start the examination into what value may lie in BUD stock, with the quant currently providing a “D+” rating which suggests a fair to slight overvaluation to be present in the stock.

While the P/E GAAP TTM of 15.7x is certainly quite high for a firm growing revenues at 1-3% per annum, it must be recalled that earnings are projected to grow in the high single digits, which, I think, should be possible as AB InBev improves their sales mix.

The P/S TTM of 2x is fair in my opinion as cyclicality in earnings should not be excessive for the brewer and once again given the real prospects of improving profitability over the coming years.

Seeking Alpha’s 1Y advanced chart for BUD also illustrates the relatively weak performance achieved by shares in 2024 with a large selloff post Q3 results resulting in a marked 16% underperformance when compared to the popular S&P 500 (SPY) index.

While quarterly results may be more susceptible to variability, the long-term outlook remains enticing and I think that additional returns could be on the horizon given the opportunity for AB InBev to spend more capital on dividends and buybacks.

After analysing these relative measures, I completed a DCF valuation model to estimate the present value of all future cashflows that would be produced by the company from today until judgement day.

I got all my levered FCF and balance sheet items from Seeking Alpha’s Financials tab for BUD. Using a trailing 10-year period, I calculated their average FCF growth rate to be 16%. While this seems impressive, it must be noted how cyclical FCF has been which tends to distort the mean of these figures.

I think a 3% future growth rate is more reasonable considering how mature and stable the business is at present and the fact that major acquisitions simply seem unrealistic for AB InBev. My discount rate is the same as the 93-year annualised average return generated by the S&P 500 index with this discount rate reflecting the opportunity cost I face should I invest in BUD stock instead of the index.

Using this data, I obtained an IV rating of $96 per share which I think is fair considering my relatively conservative forecasting estimates and implies a 38% undervaluation at present time.

When it comes to the short-term outlook (1-12 months), I suspect that AB InBev may face quite a large degree of volatility in returns due to the obscure and increasingly overpriced market conditions in the U.S.

Still, I suspect that over the next 1-10 years AB InBev is well positioned to increase the rate of shareholder returns while simultaneously continuing to adapt their product mix to changing consumer preferences and demands. This cumulatively should allow outsized returns for patient stockholders at least in my opinion.

Risks facing AB InBev

AB InBev faces two key threats in my opinion: changing consumer preferences and competitive pressures.

While cyclicality is a given for consumer discretionary companies, I believe AB InBev faces a much larger risk from changing tastes and drinking habits among consumers resulting in a material decline in beer consumption.

The increased health concerns and understanding around the dangers of alcohol consumption combined with the rising popularity of spirits and specialty beverages certainly could result in beer becoming a less popular drink.

This would of course be hugely detrimental to AB InBev as their core product portfolio is about 95% (measured by revenues and brewing volumes) beer based.

A similar situation is presented by both fierce competition and rising popularity of craft beers within the alcoholic beverage industry, as well as the aforementioned propensity for younger consumers to drink specialty beverages such as cocktails and hard seltzers.

While the popularity of beer has shifted throughout the last two centuries, even a temporary reduction in consumption could result in generations who prefer to drink other beverages.

I think the only tool AB InBev has to combat against these risks is to expand their non-beer portfolio, which the company is of course doing. However, I also think that the company’s expertise lies within the beer industry which is why expansion beyond it could be less successful both in volumes and profitability.

With these risks in mind, I award the firm a high-risk rating and would strongly suggest all readers to conduct their own research into these topics if they are of concern to you.

Summary

Anheuser-Busch InBev is a behemoth within the beer industry. While rival firms with similar quality brands do exist, none have the same economies of scale and breadth of brands that AB InBev enjoys.

Full-year results were resoundingly positive with great increases in profitability and revenues supporting the hypothesis that AB has a real pathway to producing wonderful returns for shareholders.

The valuation is enticing with a 38% undervaluation suggesting that a deep-value opportunity might just be up for grabs in BUD stock.

In lieu of these factors, I feel it is appropriate to upgrade my rating to a Strong Buy and have added an additional 20% (compared to my initial position) worth of shares to my portfolio with a view to holding them for another 5-10 years or until a resoundingly premium valuation.

data.title: Ambac: Despite EPS Miss, It's A Growth Company
data.description:
Ambac Financial Group (NYSE:AMBC) is a stock I've twice covered, and in my last thesis I rated it a Buy, believing it was making material improvements that would improve its balance sheet and allow for less-risky earnings growth going forward.

Full-year 2024 results have since come out, and it seems the market was not happy, down over 10% at open. Long-term investors, however, can look past the earnings miss and see the bigger picture; a scaling insurer that has reached underwriting profits, expansion of a capital-light distribution platform, and several other non-recurring expenses that were necessary to get through the turnaround projects of 2024.

Consequently, I maintain my confidence in the company, along with my Buy rating.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I wrote last time, AMBC had been going down in price already. There were a few things that seemed to contribute to that market sentiment.

Tangible book value had declined sharply during the year. This might have concerned the market, as insurers need assets to back their insurance obligations, but I noted this was largely attributed to their acquisition of Beat Capital, a non-risk-bearing distributor.

This was all piggy-backing on my March thesis, which focused on Ambac's transformation into the growing MGA market and finally moving out of the vestiges of its legacy business that had been weighing it down since the 2008 Financial Crisis.

The remainder of that was in the process of being sold to Oaktree Capital (OAK.PR.A)/(OAK.PR.B), and I believed that would not only be better for long-term earnings but that the cash would improve liquidity and the balance sheet. At the time, management was simply waiting for the State of Wisconsin to approve the transaction.

Because of the apparent discount due to the confusion about TBV per share's decline and the improving growth story, I rated AMBC a Buy.

Full-Year Results

As the fourth quarter and full-year 2024 was concerned, there was a lot of success in terms of improving fundamentals:

  • 88% YoY growth in Q4 specialty P&C insurance premiums
  • 93% growth in annual revenue from their distributor platform, Cirrata
  • Improvement of combined ratio by 490 BPS between 2023 and 2024

Yet, the stock is down since results came out. This likely owes to the earnings miss by a substantial margin. Non-GAAP EPS was negative and much less than guided.

The miss stems from exclusion of the positive impact of a non-controlling interest.

Ultimately, however, I think long-term investors have little reason to care about earnings misses or beats. These are arbitrary milestones, and their real interest should be the long-term earning potential of the business.

Another matter may also be that the final stamp of approval on the sale of the legacy business to Oaktree has not yet been confirmed by management. During the earnings call, they expected Wisconsin to approve it this quarter or early in the next, but it was pending even during the Q3 earnings call.

So with those issues aside, how was the improvement in profitability?

The chart above shows that, while there are quarterly fluctuations, their business is improving with scale. Moreover, I was previously concerned that Q4's storm season could show increased claims, but loss ratios were improved compared to Q3 and Q4 2023. This shows that they did a good job in 2024 of diversifying their book.

Their distribution business is also showing strong signs of growth, both organically and through their M&A. In my view, the Q4 results were not definitively bad. It just points to how turnarounds have hiccups that muddy the bigger picture.

Future Outlook

Overall, I suspect the market's negative reaction was a response to the negative earnings for Q4, paired with the lack of completion on the Oaktree deal, so let's just take a moment to consider Ambac's path to profits.

Management provides the table above as a guide to where earnings are to be improved. 2024 was a net loss of $58.9M. The P&C segment is already profitable in isolation. The distribution business and corporate side are where improvements can be found.

Thankfully, many of these expenses are non-recurring, related to the turnaround efforts of the year. This includes acquisition-related expenses and severances. The distribution segment also carried $17.6M in amortization. Ambac's adjusted net income illustrates how earnings could look without these nearly $50M items, and how benefits of scale from growth can lead to profitability.

Management believes that the current path should lead to adjusted EBITDA of $80M - $90M with these growth forces. As income taxes and interest are only a few million at the moment, I believe this also gives a good idea of the earning power of this company going forward.

With the distribution business in particular, which was largely being built out in 2024 with the acquisition of Beat Capital, I expect it will contribute disproportionately to earnings growth.

Valuation

Lacking an updated balance sheet with the release (the Form 10K is not yet filed), as well as the lack of conclusion on the sale of the legacy business, TBV per share is still something we'll need time to reevaluate. That doesn't mean we can't value AMBC. We're looking at a business with a path to becoming profitable soon and earning about $80M per year. That implies EPS of about $1.69, making today's price a fairly low P/E of 6.3.

Remember, it's not simply that the business is growing; MGA and specialty insurance markets are. This is why Ambac targeted them for their turnaround. I think it's reasonable to look at AMBC as a growth stock, and it's becoming easier to see the price double as they approach just their 2028 goals.

Risks

Given their diverse product offerings, the higher premium that specialty P&C tends to attract, and the lack of risk-bearing from the distribution segment, there's a lot of danger contained right there. Where I see an issue is in the delay of Wisconsin's approval of the Oaktree deal (it's curious that it's taken this long). This will improve their balance sheet, and the longer it hangs in the air, the longer they continue with that weakness.

The other matter is that their growth depends on favorable industry trends, for specialty and MGA. If this were to reverse, Ambac would have to turn around again before the first turnaround is quite finished. It's not clear that they possess and operation that allows them to pivot seamlessly.

Conclusion

Ambac achieved a lot of fundamental improvements during 2024, and I do not believe these were reflected in the market's pricing. Using a little elbow grease to turn a company around is seldom pretty, and hyper-fixation on things such as earnings beats or misses can skew the picture.

Management has delivered on creating a positive combined ratio and growing its distribution segment. It is fundamentally different from its days of writing guarantee policies. At this point, it's largely a question of scaling, and as that moment approaches, I expect the market will feel differently again. That is why I rate it a Buy.

data.title: Oklo: Pioneering Advanced Nuclear Solutions For The AI Era
data.description:
The global energy sector is rushing full steam ahead towards cleaner, more reliably, and highly-scalable power solutions. One company representing this huge task is Oklo (NYSE:OKLO), which could play a crucial role in meeting the increasing demand from AI-driven data centres, surging industrial production, and efforts in meeting clean energy targets. The firm was founded in 2013 by two MIT graduates, Jacobs DeWitte and Caroline Cochran, with the aim to go against the more traditional nuclear power model, instead focussing on the enormous potential of small, but highly-efficient, fast reactors. These also have the benefit of recycling nuclear waste, which addresses another enormous issue which has plagued the nuclear industry since inception.

As the energy market continues to evolve, I sense that Oklo could be an interesting opportunity for investors. Clearly renewable solutions are the more appetising for most, but with grid reliability and demand consistently proving to be a core issue, I see there being a key role going forward for companies able to provide reliable baseload power while also minimising the waste and pollution risk most traditional power stations have to contend with.

Oklo's Technology and Model

The company's primary offering is the Aurora powerhouse, a small-scale fast reactor with the ability to generate an impressive 50 MW of power. Unlike most nuclear reactors, it can operate for up to ten years without the need for refuelling, a costly, risky, and disruptive process. By the company perfecting this process over time, it is able to effectively produce these reactors in a factor setting rather than on-site, meaning it can deploy enormous savings in scalability and other synergies. It can then operate in a flexible model to build, own, and operate these reactors, enabling excellent revenue streams while keeping control of costs and risks.

Such a model also delivers predictable cash flow and a reliable income stream, unlike traditional utility providers which rely on fluctuating energy prices in the market. The reactors are designed specifically to meet the needs of data centres, and other AI infrastructure which requires a continuous, low-cost source of power. This could position it as a key enabler for the digital economy going forward.

Strategic Partnerships Look Promising

To make this revolution in small-scale reactors work, the company clearly needs to hit the ground running with finding business. In December 2024, the firm entered into a Master Power Agreement with Switch Inc. a major operator of data centres. This deal outlines a process of deploying as much as 12GW of power through to 2044, making it one of the largest clean energy deals in history. Then there's a deal with RPower to link with natural gas solutions, ensuring data centres have a reliable source of power while full-scale nuclear solutions and developed steadily. While this is still a hybrid approach, it represents how flexible the operating model of the business can be when required.

Powering business is clearly going to be the primary revenue stream, but it should not be underestimated how much potential there is in fuel recycling. Earlier this month, the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) approved the company's plans for the Aurora Fuel Fabrication Facility, enabling a range of recycled fuel technologies, and ensuring a secure supply of nuclear fuel for future projects.

Obviously, regulation is going to play a major role in the success of all projects which the company is looking towards, but by building relationships with government bodies, and working with legislators, the company can ensure it positions itself as a thought leader in the industry, paving the way for success while adhering to and ensuring a high quality of regulation.

Opportunities and Challenges Ahead

An investment in such a dynamic space is always going to be a fairly turbulent one, but for the right kind of investor, with a clear understanding of the catalysts and drivers which will define the success of the company, I think there's a lot to like.

For starters, the backing of various government and legislative incentive programmes, including the ADVANCE Act, could accelerate many of the company's deployment timelines. Not many firms are in a position to effectively strike while the iron is hot in the nuclear sector, so Oklo has a real shot at first-mover advantage.

On the flip side, there are plenty of reasons why I think all investors need to be cautious here. With an entirely sensible amount of regulation in place, the nuclear industry can be notoriously difficult to move quickly in, meaning that approvals for new designs and licencing can be costly. Obviously such a business is capital-intensive by its nature, but since the company is still in loss-making territory at $24.9 million in Q3 2024, there are plenty of risks at this stage of development.

If customers look to alternative forms of energy production, for example hydrogen, and deploy advanced battery storage as a bridging solution, there could be disappointment for investors. I see the success of the next few years being heavily dependent on management scaling operations efficiently, and securing further long-term purchase agreements.

Valuation and Analyst Perspectives

Despite being a fairly early stage company, it now holds an impressive market capitalisation of over $4 billion. With the shares up over 200% in a year, investor confidence is understandably high, with notable figures such as Sam Altman of OpenAI and Cathie Wood of Ark Invest providing support for the company.

Given the current fundamentals of the company, there is understandable reason for some caution, where an enormous rally can lead to profit-taking and volatility, but Wall Street ratings and price targets suggest there is plenty more to like over the coming years.

Evaluating the fair value of such a company is naturally a challenge, since the market capitalisation suggests an enormous operation, but with only 78 employees, and no sign of profits, there is still a lot of growth to do. This seems to be the only blemish for the Quant Rating of the company though, with a decent performance across the valuation, growth rates and momentum.

One to Watch

With demand surging for clean energy to fuel the AI revolution, I see Oklo having plenty of opportunities to succeed over the coming years. With impressive technology, some huge partnerships, and encouraging regulation, many investors will see the path to success ahead. There are admittedly plenty of risks for any company yet to make a profit, but with a long-term horizon, and appropriate risk management, I'd say the sector looks promising. I wouldn't go far to push the buy button just yet, since the recent rally has likely baked in a lot of the positive catalysts, but will be watching this one carefully as it moves closer towards profitability.

data.title: How U.S.-EU Relations Could Impact The Cross Currency Basis
data.description:
By Chris Farran

At a Glance

  • Policy changes could impact the historically cooperative relationship between the Federal Reserve and the European Central Bank (ECB)
  • The CME EUR/USD Cross Currency Basis Index tracks the nearest forward starting 3-month IMM period and can provide insight into relative changes in demand for either USD or EUR

As discussions around U.S.-EU relations move to the fore, various unknowns are still ahead. As market participants assess what could be next, it may be worth keeping an eye on CME Group's EUR/USD Cross Currency Basis Index (XCCY). This index measures the basis point deviation from covered interest rate parity in the EUR/USD foreign exchange market.

Assessing Market Alignment

The Euro Short-Term Rate (€STR) and Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR) not only provide the interest rate used to borrow money in their respective regions, but the spread between the two can reveal trends or preferences emerging in the foreign exchange market.

Since June of last year, the Three Month SOFR-€STR spread (measured by their respective nearby forward starting quarterly-IMM dated CME Group futures contracts) has both narrowed from 175bp to 100bp and subsequently re-widened to over 200bp. A higher spread is partly a function of Central Bank policy, but may also imply that USD liquidity is tighter than euro liquidity; a lower spread, the opposite.

During the same period, the X-CCY Basis Index has ranged from -13 bps to 2 bps. A X-CCY Basis Index value near zero means that interest rate markets and FX markets are aligned in their expectations of forward FX prices. As of February 7, 2025, the XCCY Basis Index was -0.49 - just a half basis point away from zero.

The daily correlation between these two measurements is 0.753 over this period, suggesting a moderately strong relationship between the SOFR-€STR spread widening and the XCCY Basis Index narrowing. Monitoring the X-CCY Basis Index could give insight into potential risks or arbitrage opportunities tied to the SOFR-€STR spread.

Policy Impacts?

Any change in U.S. and EU trade relations could affect euro and U.S. dollar money markets, and therefore, the EUR/USD cross currency basis. If policies create tension between inflation and economic growth in both the U.S. and the EU, it could make it harder for the respective monetary policymakers to set the level of short-term interest rates that would both contain inflation and enable economic growth.

In addition, there has historically been close cooperation between the Federal Reserve and the European Central Bank (ECB). For instance, the two central banks activated their existing USD swap line to provide liquidity during the initial days of the COVID-19 pandemic. A shifting economic posture between the U.S. and EU has the potential to re-write the rules of Fed-ECB relations.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tyra Biosciences: FGFR1/2 Driven AE Data Spooked The Market
data.description:
I covered Tyra Biosciences (NASDAQ:TYRA) two years ago. Tyra went up a lot in anticipation of data from the lead molecule, but then dropped like a stone when the data was announced.

Tyra develops molecules targeting the fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR). Its lead asset is TYRA-300, which targets FGFR3 and will start a phase 2 trial in Achondroplasia. It will also begin another phase 2 trial in Bladder and solid tumors. Among these solid tumors, a phase 2 trial for NMIBC has been cleared by the FDA. They have two other molecules, TYRA-200, an oral FGFR1/2/3, and TYRA-430, an FGFR4/3-biased inhibitor.

TYRA-300 is the first oral FGFR3 inhibitor in the clinic. There are other FGFR inhibitors, but these are pan-FGFR inhibitors which cause significant side-effects. That may necessitate dose interruptions and reductions. Also, such non-selective inhibition contributes to the development of acquired resistance, reducing the durability of the drug's effects.

The side effects associated with pan-FGFR inhibitors are primarily due to their off-target activity:

  • FGFR1 inhibition: Disrupts phosphate metabolism, leading to hyperphosphatemia (elevated phosphate levels in the blood), a common adverse event associated with FGFR inhibitors.

  • FGFR2 inhibition: Causes dermatologic adverse events, including nail toxicities and stomatitis (inflammation of the mouth).

  • FGFR4 inhibition: May increase the risk of liver toxicity due to the blockade of FGF19 signaling through FGFR4.

On the other hand, FGFR3 inhibition has been associated with bone growth effects, particularly in pediatric populations.

The problem with developing a selective FGFR3 targeting molecule is that all these FGFR family members have high structural similarity. Tyra utilized subtle structural differences within the FGFR family to develop the FGFR3-selective TYRA-300.

Lead program is urothelial carcinoma, a type of bladder cancer, presenting a significant unmet medical need. Each year, approximately 40,000 patients in the United States are diagnosed with urothelial cancers driven by FGFR3 alterations. Current therapies benefit only a fraction of these patients, often with limited treatment duration. By offering a more precise and better-tolerated alternative, TYRA-300 aims to expand treatment options and improve outcomes for a larger patient population.

Tested in a rat model, TYRA-300 saw a dose dependent reduction in plasma phosphate levels, while erdafitinib, a pan-FGFR inhibitor, saw a dose dependent increase in plasma phosphate levels. I also noted earlier that TYRA-300 demonstrated very high tumor volume reduction compared to erdafitinib; up to 77% versus only 12% for erdafitinib in one such model.

Two years ago, all we had was preclinical data, and that was very promising. Phase 1 data from this bladder cancer program was anticipated in 2024, and the stock went up considerably in anticipation. However, the day they announced that data from the SURF301 trial, on October 24, 2024, the stock dropped 50% on abnormally high volume.

The data

Very broadly, the efficacy data was better than or comparable to erdafitinib, but the market got spooked by the safety data - arguably needlessly. In the safety data, a rare few AEs were seen that were associated with FGFR1 or FGFR2. If we compare those with erdafitinib, as the company has done, we can easily see that there is simply no comparison between the two datasets. TYRA-300 is without a shred of doubt the much, much safer drug. However, those FGFR1/2 driven AEs, however few, must have demonstrated to the market that TYRA-300 isn’t as selective of FGFR3 as it claimed to be. That would be one way of looking at it.

However, if you look at the stock movement immediately before and after the data announcement, perhaps a different interpretation comes to mind. It is this: the stock went up in anticipation of a far superior data than was announced. The data that came out was very good, but not perfect. The market was expecting perfect data - full and complete FGFR3 selectivity and its associated efficacy/safety profile. That didn’t happen, so the market corrected itself. But the stock didn’t fall drastically from before the pre-correction levels. It found itself a bottom that wasn’t too far from when the stock went up. So the market is basically giving Tyra another opportunity to prove itself - perfectly. This is how I read the price movement.

On page 27 of this data presentation, Tyra presents a side by side comparison of the AE profiles of the two molecules. The data clearly suggests (to me, at least) that TYRA-300 is more tolerable, with fewer high-grade (≥ Grade 3) toxicities. For FGFR2-driven side effects, what we see here is that TYRA-300 shows a much lower overall incidence of nail disorders and stomatitis (7%) compared to erdafitinib (70% and 56%, respectively). For TYRA-300, all reported FGFR2-related toxicities are either Grade 1 or 2, with only one case each of Grade 3 nail disorders and stomatitis. Erdafitinib, however, has a high proportion of Grade 3 or higher cases for nail disorders, stomatitis, and PPEs. Also note that no cases of central serous retinopathy (a vision-related issue) were reported for TYRA-300, whereas erdafitinib had an 18% incidence.

Coming to FGFR1-driven AEs, Hyperphosphatemia is a major issue with erdafitinib (76% incidence), whereas TYRA-300 has a much lower rate (13%), with no Grade 3 cases.

There are also a number of unknown-driver side effects, where too, TYRA-300 demonstrates a much better profile. For example, Diarrhea is much more common with erdafitinib (63%) compared to TYRA-300 (20%). Liver enzyme elevations (ALT/AST increases) occur at similar rates in both groups, but TYRA-300 still has a lower incidence of Grade 3 elevations. Dry skin is reported less frequently in TYRA-300 patients (13%) compared to erdafitinib (27%), with no Grade 3 cases in the TYRA-300 group.

In terms of efficacy, TYRA-300 has demonstrated a 54.5% (6/11) confirmed PR rate vs. 35.3% ORR for erdafitinib - although the trial population for the latter was much higher. However, erdafitinib also has a clear overall survival benefit, extending median survival to one year and reducing the risk of death by 36% compared to chemotherapy; TYRA-300, still in early trials, does not have OS data.

Financials

TYRA has a market cap of $632mn and a cash balance of $360mn. R&D expenses were $22.7mn while G&A expenses were $5.9mn. At that rate, they have a cash runway of 11-12 quarters.

If we consider the FCF, their CFO is ($17.9mn) and capex is ($0.1mn), so FCF is -$17.8mn. Therefore, this calculation gives them an even longer cash runway of 19-20 quarters.

Risks

Obviously, had TYRA demonstrated good efficacy along with complete absence of FGFR1/2-driven AEs, the stock would have kept going up, and not down like it did. That would have absolutely derisked the stock, while also taking it beyond reach. The so-so data has brought it back down to reachable levels, however, its risk still remains. Erdafitinib is developed by big pharma (Janssen), but for Tyra, they bet everything on a single program, so if it collapses, the company is gone. That risk still remains, TYRA-300 not having produced the perfect data the market was looking for.

Bottom line

It is difficult to say whether the market reaction was an overreaction, or efficient. We have to wait for more data to gauge that. Even if TYRA manages to retain this level of safety - retain the same kind of AE profile - and improve the efficacy data, I think they are good. I am going to wait for topline data from the SURF301 trial before I take a call.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Small Asia-Based Names List, As TV Adtech MNTN And Others Join The Pipeline
data.description:
Four IPOs and four SPACs listed this week. Four IPOs and two SPACs submitted initial filings.

Chinese steel fittings manufacturer Luda Technology Group (LUD) priced its US IPO at the proposed price to raise $10 million at a $90 million market cap. The company manufactures stainless steel and carbon steel flanges and fittings products, and trades steel pipes, valves, and other steel tubing products. The company's sales network spans China and other parts of Asia, South America, Australia, Europe, and North America, with customers comprising manufacturers and traders from the chemical, petrochemical, maritime, and manufacturing industries. Luda Technology Group finished the week up 2%.

Basel Medical Group (BMGL), which operates two medical clinics in Singapore, priced its US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $9 million at a $74 million market cap. The company provides a range of general and subspecialized orthopedic, trauma and sports medicine services such as knee/hip replacements, sports medicine/surgery, spine surgery, foot/ankle surgery and minimally invasive orthopedic procedures, as well as neurosurgical treatments. Basel Medical Group finished the week up 4%.

Chinese charter bus company Webus International (WETO) priced its US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $8 million at an $88 million market cap. Webus offers packaged tour services, customized chartered bus services, and commuter shuttle services in China. It finished the week down 6%.

Chinese ad services provider LZ Technology (LZMH) priced its US IPO at the bottom of the range to raise $7 million at a $607 million market cap. LZ Technology places advertisements on monitors and within its SaaS software across approximately 120 cities in China. The company also connects local businesses with consumers via online promotions and transactions, and provides building access and safety management systems. LZ Technology finished the week up 41%.

Four SPACs priced this week. Fifth Era Acquisition I (FERAU), formed by Fifth Era and Blockchain Coinvestors, raised $200 million to target technology. Rithm Capital-backed Rithm Acquisition (RAC.U) raised $200 million to target financial services and real estate. Live Oak Acquisition V (LOKVU), formed by Live Oak Merchant Partners, raised $200 million. NewHold Investment III (NHICU), formed by NewHold Enterprises, raised $175 million to target industrial technology.

Four IPOs submitted initial filings, led by TV-based advertising software platform MNTN (MNTN), which filed to raise $100 million. Canada-based hydroponic growing systems provider Pinnacle Food (PFAI) filed to raise $10 million. China-based property management services provider Dalu International (DLHZ) filed to raise $8 million at a $98 million market cap. Hong Kong-based digital marketing firm Everbright Digital (EDHL) filed to raise $7 million at a $119 million market cap.

Two SPACs submitted initial filings this week. Republic Digital Acquisition (RDAGU) filed to raise $220 million to target fintech, software, and cryptocurrency. Quartzsea Acquisition (QSEAU) filed to raise $60 million to target businesses in underpenetrated markets.

Week Ahead

The March IPO market is set to start off with a quiet week. One sizable issuer is scheduled to list, though other smaller names may join the calendar late.

Medical device firm Kestra Medical Technologies (KMTS) plans to raise $150 million at a $716 million market cap. The company makes a wearable cardioverter defibrillator for patients at elevated risk of sudden cardiac arrest. Since approval, Kestra's system has been prescribed by over 550 hospitals across the US and has been worn by over 17,000 patients. The company has delivered explosive growth but is highly unprofitable.

Inflammation biotech Apimeds Pharmaceuticals US (APUS) is tentatively scheduled to raise $20 million at a $58 million market cap. The company is developing Apitox, an intradermally administered bee venom-based toxin, to treat inflammation and pain management symptoms associated with knee osteoarthritis.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 2/27/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was down 4.6% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was down 0.2%. Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kenvue (KVUE) and Arm Holdings (ARM). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 10.0% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 6.7%. Renaissance Capital's International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Porsche.
